chunks.title: Introduction to APOE4 and Chris Hemsworth
chunks.summary: The video opens with a discussion about Chris Hemsworth's genetics, particularly focusing on his APOE4 status and its implications for Alzheimer's risk. The speaker introduces how this topic gained attention through a Discovery Channel series featuring Hemsworth.
chunks.transcript: while we're talking about my genetics I do want to talk about apoe4 because this is really interesting and I think relevant in light of what's happened to Chris Hemsworth recently so if you're not familiar with this story Chris Hemsworth is part of a Discovery Channel series looking or Disney I forget who's making this series looking at how to live a long time and as part of that he got his genetics done and he didn't know the

chunks.title: Understanding APOE4 Risk
chunks.summary: The speaker explains that while having the APOE4 variant does increase the risk of Alzheimer's, it's essential to consider lifestyle, genetics, and individual context. They emphasize that many people with this variant do not develop the disease if they maintain a healthy lifestyle.
chunks.transcript: results of those genetics so Peter Atia in dramatic fashion tells him the results and Chris Hemsworth it turns out is apoe44 so Chris asks in his accent what is the risk of Alzheimer's and Peter says eight to ten times increased risk I did a reel on Instagram about this because I wanted to fill this in a little bit it is true that if you look across the population those with two copies of apoe4 and their genetics do have an increased risk of Alzheimer's but what this negates or neglects is individuality and context Western medicine is not good at understanding context what do I mean by this there's really good evidence that apoe44 doesn't necessarily mean you get Alzheimer's I would say 30 based on the statistics I've seen 30 to 55 of people with apoe44 develop Alzheimer's that's a big number but the flip side is that 70 to 45 percent of people with apoe44 don't get Alzheimer's so what's happening in the people with ablee44 that don't get Alzheimer's there are other things at play perhaps genetics perhaps lifestyle and environment so what I wish Peter had told Chris was and this wouldn't make for as good television because Nuance is hard to communicate to an audience what I wish Peter had told Chris was the general population with this who doesn't think about what they eat doesn't think about the way they live is it an eight to tens and times increased risk of Alzheimer's you Chris Hemsworth if you think about what you eat if you live well and you are not insulin resistant and I'll get to why I say that in a moment you probably have a much lower risk of Alzheimer's even with

chunks.title: Historical Context of APOE Variants
chunks.summary: The discussion shifts to the historical perspective of APOE variants, noting that the APOE4 variant was prevalent among early humans for hundreds of thousands of years. The speaker points out the surprising resilience of APOE4 in certain populations and its potential protective effects against cognitive decline.
chunks.transcript: I have IPO e33 so I am part of the population that has this more recent uh genetics regarding apoe what do I mean by that so here's the deal with APO e this is an apolipoprotein that is involved in cholesterol transport glucose transport in the brain and appears to be very affected by insulin resistance in a negative way but historically apoe4 was the variant that prehominids and humans had for hundreds of thousands of years it appears that apoe3 only arose 200 000 years ago and apoe two about eighty thousand years ago this means for the majority of our Evolution as Homo habilis Homo erectus pre-hominids we were all apoe44 and Homo sapiens appears to have arrived on the scene about 350 400 000 years ago so perhaps for half or close to half of our existence as Homo sapiens everyone your mom your grandmother your brother your sister your dad they were all you were all apoe44 this genetic variant clearly had some benefit for us so we don't really know why apoe3 arose but there is good evidence that in indigenous populations of people like the cement t-s-i-m-a-n-e from Bolivia and the Yoruba from Nigeria apoe44 is actually a protective variant when it comes to cognitive decline in the elderly yes I said that in populations around the world apoe44 is protective against cognitive decline here's an article for those watching on videos apollypoprotein E4 is associated with improved cognitive function and Amazonian forager horticulturalists with a high parasite burden is apoe4 protective when they have a high parasite burden who knows but you can see here that they say being an E4 carrier is the strongest risk factor to date of Alzheimer's disease and cognitive decline in industrial populations it is associated with greater cognitive performance and individuals facing a high parasite burden and pathogen load suggesting advantages to the E4 allele

chunks.title: The Role of Insulin Sensitivity
chunks.summary: The importance of insulin sensitivity is highlighted as a major factor in determining health outcomes related to genetics. The speaker argues that Western medicine often overlooks the context of individual health and emphasizes the need for maintaining healthy insulin levels to mitigate risks associated with genetics.

chunks.title: Saturated Fat and Alzheimer's Disease
chunks.summary: The video delves into the controversial topic of saturated fat consumption, questioning the existing links between saturated fat and Alzheimer's risk in APOE4 carriers. The speaker argues that the relationship is often confounded by unhealthy lifestyle choices rather than the fat itself.
